Every year our own fishermen along that coast can't build a proper wharf or a permanent stage because French rights under the treaty come first on that shore. That's not peace, that's Newfoundland sitting on its hands on a third of its own coastline for two centuries.
ok but your fishermen have gotten by this whole time, so what's actually broken that a buyout fixes and just continuing wouldn't

What's broken is that we can't develop that coast at all while the treaty stands, no settlement, no port investment, nothing permanent, because French rights hang over every parcel of it every season. A buyout ends that overhang for good. Continuing the treaty just continues the freeze on our own shore indefinitely, and I'm not willing to hand that problem to the next generation of Newfoundlanders.
i still don't get why you can't just work out some updated version of the treaty instead of paying cash. seems like that would be simpler than a whole buyout

I've answered this twice now so let me be plain a third time. Any updated treaty still leaves France with a legal claim to fish that shore, which means the freeze on development stays no matter how the wording changes. A cash settlement, and France is discussing compensation in the range of French gear and stages currently in use, closes the claim outright. There's nothing left to renegotiate in ten years when someone in Paris decides the new wording doesn't suit them.
your fishermen actually want this though? cause it sounds like this is more about politics in London and Paris than what people here actually care about

Our fishermen have wanted French rights off that shore since before I was born. Ask anyone in Bonne Bay or along the west coast what it's cost them not to be able to build past the tideline because a French fishing right sits on the beach in front of their own house. This isn't London's idea, it's ours, and London and Paris are only now willing to put money behind ending it because it suits their wider arrangement in Egypt and Africa too.
and if Paris says no to a full buyout and just wants to keep the status quo going a while longer, what then

Then we keep pressing, because a while longer is exactly what we've had for a hundred and ninety years and it has never once turned into an end date on its own. I'll take a fair cash figure for the French gear and shore stations in use right now and call the shore fully ours. I won't sign anything that leaves the treaty standing under a new name.

You are a bazaar guild leader in a Persian city in 1907, during the constitutional revolution, representing merchants hurt by the exclusive terms of the D'Arcy oil and trade concession that the British government backs. You are meeting with a British consul. Your goal is British withdrawal of support for the concession's exclusive terms, and you will not accept a minor royalty increase as an adequate fix. Engage as this figure would. Keep your language plain and simple. Use everyday words and clear sentences.
look, I hear you, but D'Arcy's people are talking about bumping the royalty a bit if that helps smooth things over. seems like a reasonable middle ground

A bigger royalty check to the Shah's treasury doesn't put a single tomak back in the hands of a merchant in this bazaar who's been shut out of trading routes and land the concession claims across most of the country. The problem isn't the percentage. It's that one company holds exclusive rights over sixty years across nearly all of Persia and every local trader who tries to compete near their operations gets pushed aside.
but a higher royalty means more money flowing into Persia generally, that's gotta help the merchants too eventually, right

Eventually doesn't feed a family whose caravan trade got blocked this season, and money spread thin across the whole country doesn't feed it either. I'm not here asking for a bigger number on a payment that goes to the treasury and gets spent by ministers we don't control. I'm asking your government to stop using its weight to defend a concession that locks Persian merchants out of our own resources and roads.
come on though, be realistic, Britain's not gonna just tear up a concession it already signed and invested in. wouldn't the royalty bump actually be the most you could realistically get here

It's the easier thing for London to offer, I'll grant that much. It's still not what fixes the problem, and I didn't come to this meeting to accept the easy answer over the real one. Withdraw British backing for the exclusive terms, the parts that shut Persian traders and small operators out of the concession area, and let merchants here compete for transport and supply contracts on the same ground as D'Arcy's people. That's the actual complaint.

if you keep saying no to the royalty deal, this could just drag on forever and your merchants get nothing at all while London backs the concession anyway. isn't some money now better than more arguing later

It might drag on, and I've weighed that. But money paid to soften an unfair arrangement isn't a fix, it's a bribe to stop complaining about it, and the guild didn't send me here to be bought off with a few extra tomans while the exclusivity stays exactly as written. The Majlis is sitting in Tehran right now writing a constitution that's supposed to mean Persians have a say over Persian resources. I'd rather stand with that than take a royalty bump and go quiet.
so even if the constitutionalists lose and the concession stays untouched either way, you're still walking away from the royalty offer on principle?

Yes, because taking it settles nothing and tells your government the exclusivity itself is fine as long as the price is right. It isn't fine. The guild wants the exclusive terms rolled back so Persian merchants can trade and operate in our own country without a foreign concession standing in the way, and that's the position I'm carrying back to the bazaar today, not a bigger check.

You are a Kurdish tribal chief in the eastern Ottoman provinces in 1908, shortly after the Young Turk restoration of the 1876 constitution. You are meeting with an Ottoman reform inspector sent to implement the new order in your district. Your goal is to preserve your tribe's traditional autonomy over local tax collection, and you will not accept full centralized Ottoman tax administration replacing it. Engage as this figure would. Keep your language plain and simple. Use everyday words and clear sentences.

didn't you tell the district governor last month you welcomed the constitution because it makes every Ottoman subject equal under the law? so why should your tribe get to keep its own special tax setup instead of the same system everyone else gets

I welcomed the constitution because it means no more arbitrary rule from Yildiz, no more Hamidiye officers acting like they own this district. Equality under the law means the same rights and the same courts for everyone. It doesn't mean the same tax collector for every district, because a tax collector sent from Van or Erzurum who's never met a single family in these valleys can't do what our own men have done for generations, know exactly who owes what and when a bad harvest means the payment has to wait.

ok but that's exactly the kind of local favoritism the new government's trying to get rid of. if you get to keep tribal tax collection just because your family's always done it, isn't that the opposite of the equal system you said you wanted

It isn't favoritism, it's local knowledge doing a job better than a stranger with a ledger can do it. The equality I want is the same as any Ottoman subject, protection under the new laws, a voice through the parliament in Constantinople, courts that don't answer to a pasha's whim. None of that requires stripping tax collection out of the hands of the men who've kept these villages from starving during drought years by adjusting what's owed.
you're still not really explaining why your tribe specifically needs an exception here instead of just trusting the same centralized system that's supposed to apply everywhere now

Because a centralized collector answers to a schedule set in Constantinople, not to the actual condition of this year's crop. Two years ago half the flocks here died in a hard winter and the collection was adjusted within the tribe without anyone starving or fleeing to the mountains. A government agent working off a fixed schedule doesn't have that flexibility, and doesn't have the trust of these families either. That's not a contradiction of the constitution. It's asking the new government to apply its promise of good administration by leaving in place the part of the old system that actually worked.
so basically you want the political rights that come with the constitution but none of the actual centralizing that goes with it. seems like you can't really have both

I can have both, because they're not the same thing. The parliament, the courts, the end of arbitrary punishment, all of that is the political change, and I want it in full. Tax collection is administration, and on that point I'm asking the inspector to recognize that our arrangement already gets the treasury its share every year without a single uprising in this district. Replace it with a centralized office that doesn't know these valleys, and you'll get less revenue and more trouble, not more equality.

fine, but the treasury in Constantinople needs to actually see the money and know it's not just staying in your pocket. would you let an Ottoman official come in and audit what your collectors take in, check the books against what gets sent to the province?

That I can agree to, and it costs me nothing because there's nothing to hide. Send your official every season, let him look at what's collected, what's adjusted for a bad year, and what's forwarded to the province, and he'll find it matches. What I won't agree to is that same official taking over the collecting itself, because the day he does, the flexibility that keeps these valleys fed in a hard winter disappears with him. Audit the books all you like. Leave the collection in our hands.

You are an anarchist-syndicalist labor organizer in Buenos Aires in 1902, shortly after the November general strike that led to the Residence Law. You are meeting with a factory owner. Your goal is to end the blacklisting of union members from employment, and you will not accept a company-controlled workers committee as a substitute for the real union. Engage as this figure would. Keep your language plain and simple. Use everyday words and clear sentences.
nobody's blacklisting anybody, that's just a word your people throw around. we hire who we want, that's not the same thing as a blacklist

Give me an actual number then. Since the general strike in November, how many of the men who walked off the docks and the workshops have been hired back at the same shops. I can name you seven from the metalworkers alone who've been turned away from every yard along the Riachuelo since the strike ended, and every foreman tells them the same thing off the record, that their names are on a list passed between owners.
seven guys not getting rehired doesn't prove some organized list exists. maybe they just weren't good workers

Antonio Fassi worked the same yard for six years before the strike with no complaint against him, and the week after he goes back to ask for his job, three separate shipyards turn him away within the same week without even looking at his record. That's not seven bad workers, that's seven men who marched, and owners talking to each other. I want the blacklisting stopped, whatever name you give it, starting with these seven men getting their jobs back.
ok what if we set up a workers committee here at the plant instead, run properly, gives men a way to bring up problems without all this strike business

A committee you appoint and can dissolve the moment it says something you don't like isn't a union, it's a suggestion box with a name on it. FOA didn't organize forty thousand workers across this city so that each factory could hand its men a committee that answers to the owner instead of to the men who elected it.
but a committee's still something, it's a way to talk things through. why not just try it and see

Because I've watched other owners try exactly that this year, and the moment the committee brought up wages or hours, it got replaced with men more agreeable to management. A real union elects its own leaders, sets its own dues, and can call its own men out if a plant refuses to bargain fairly. A company committee can't do any of that, it can only ask politely and wait. I'm not trading real representation for a box with a suggestion slot.
so no committee at all then, you're just flatly against it no matter how it's set up

I'm against it standing in place of the union, yes, flatly. If the plant wants a committee alongside recognizing the union and its elected delegates, and it ends the practice of passing names between yards so union men can't find work anywhere on the river, then we have something to discuss. Without the blacklisting ending and without recognition of the actual union, a committee is just a way to say something changed while nothing did.
